1. Localised - Limited to the gingiva adjacent to a single tooth or
group of Teeth
2. Generalised - involving the gingiva throughout the mouth
3. Marginal - confined to the marginal gingiva
4. Papillary - confined to the interdental papilla
- involving the marginal and attached gingiva and

c> BASED ON DEGREE OF GINGIVAL ENLARGEMENT
1. Grade 0 - No signs of gingival enlargement
2. Grade 1 - Enlargement confined to interdental papillae
3. Grade2 - Enlargement involves papilla and marginal gingiva
4. Grade 4- - Enlargement covers three quarters or more of crown

DRUG INDUCED GINGIVAL ENLARGEMENT
Gingival enlargement is a well-known consequence of the administration of
some anticonvulsants, calcium-chonnel blockers and îmmunasuppressant.
These drugs inhibit intracellular calcium ion influx, so the action of these drugs
on Na & Ca influx may be the reason for these dissimilar drugs to have a
common side effect upon a secondary target tissue i.e gingiva.
In anticonvulsants, Phenytoin is the most common drug which causes GE.
Other drugs which causes GE are Ethasuccimide and Valproic acid.
In immunasuppressants, cyclasporine is the most common. Other drug which
causes GE is Tacrolimus.
In Calcium channel blockers, nifedipine is the most common while other
drugs are Verapamil and Diltiazem.
CLINICAL FEATURES
The enlargement starts as a painless, bead-Like growth of the interdental papilla
and extends to the facial and Lingual gingival margins.
As the condition progresses, the marginal and papillary enlargements unite
and may develop into a massive tissue fold covering considerable partion of
crown & may interfere with occlusion.
When uncomplicated by inflammation, the lesion is mulberry shaped, firm, pale
pink, and resilient, with a minutely lobulated surface & no tendency to bleed.
The presence of enlargement makes plaque control difficult, often resulting in a
secondary inflammatory process that complicates the overgrowth caused by the
drug.
Secondary inflammatory changes produce a red or bluish red discolaration,
obliterate the lobulated surface demarcations and increased bleeding tendency
The enlargement is usually generalised throughout the mouth but is more
severe in the maxillary and mandibular anterior region.
It occurs in areas in which teethare present, not in edentulous spaces, & the
enlargement disappears in areas from which Teeth are extracted.
Drug induced GE may occur in mouths with Little or no plaque & may be
absents in mouths with abundant deposits.
The enlargement is chronic & slowly increases in size. When surgically
removed, it recurs. Spontaneous disappearance occurs within months of
discontinuation of the drug.

HISTOPATHOLOGY
Drug induced GE consists of a pronounced hyperplasia of the connective tissue
and epithelium.
There is acanthasis of the epithelium, and elongated rete pegs extending deep
into connective tissue, which exhibits densely arranged collagen bundles
with an increase in the number of fibroblasts & new blood vessels.
Structural changes in the outer epithelial cell surface have been reported
in cyclasparine induced enlargements.
An inflammatory infiltrate may be found at the bottom of the sulcus.
Cyclasporine enlargements usually have a more highly vascularised
c.t with foci of chronic inflammatory cells, particularly plasma cells.
The mature Phenytoin enlargement has a sibroblost/collagen.ratio equal
to that of normal gingiva.
Recurring phenytoin enlargements appear as granulation tissue composed
of numerous young capillaries & fibroblasts & irregularly arranged
collagen fibrils.

GINGIVA
Gingiva is the part of the oral mucosa that covers the alveolar process
of the jaws and surrounds the necks of the Teeth.
CLASSIFICATION
1> MARGINAL GINGIVA
It is the Terminal edge or barder of the gingiva surrounding the teeth
in a collar like fashion.
In about 50% of cases ,it is demarcated from adjacent attached gingiva
by a shallow Linear depression, called free gingival groove.
It is about 1 mm wide & forms soft tissue wall of gingival sulcus.
2> ATTACHED GINGIVA
The attached gingiva is continuous with the marginal gingiva and is
firm, resilient and is tightly bound to the underlying periasteum
of alveolar bone:
The facial aspect of the attached gingiva extends upto the alveolar
mucasa and is demarcated by the mucogingival junction
Width of the adjacent attached gingiva is the distance between the
mucogingival junction and the projection on the external surface
of the bottom of the gingival sulcus or periodontal pocket.
Width is greatest in incisor region
3.5-4.5 mm in maxilla
3.3-3.9 mm in mandible
It is least in pasterior segments
1.9 snam in maxilla
1.8 mm in mandible
The width increases with age and in supra erupted Teeth
3> INTERDENTAL GINGIVA
The interdental gingiva occupies the gingival embrasure, which is
the interproximal space beneath the area of tooth contact.
It can be pyramidal or col shaped
The col shaped interdental gingiva presents a valleylike depression
that connects the facial and Lingual papilla and conforms to the
shape of the interproximal contact.

GINGIVAL EPITHELIUM
Gingival epithelium is studied under the following headings
Gingival Epithelium
Layers of GE
Cells of GE
Gingival Fluid
Oral Epithelium
Sulcular Epithelium Keratinocytes
Non-keratinocytes
Junctional Epithelium
Melanocytes
Langerhans cells
Merkelcells
The GE consists of a continuous Lining of stratified squamous epithelium.
The main function of GE is to protect the deep structures, while allowing
a selective interchange with the oral environment. This is achieved by
proliferation and differentiation of the keratinocytes.
OUTER/ORAL EPITHELIUM
Covers the crest and outersurface of the marginal gingiva and the surface
of the attached gingiva
Average thickness is 0.2-0.3 mm.
If is keratinised or parakeratinised
It is composed of 4 Layers - a> stratum basale
b> stratum spinosum
c> stratum granulasum
d> stratum corneum
The degree of gingival keratinisation diminishes with age and onset of
menopause.
SULCULAR EPITHELIUM
It lines the gingival sulcus.
It is thin, non keratinised stratified squamous epithelium without rete peg
It extends from caronal limit of the junctional epithelium to the crest of
the gingival margin.
It is devoid of stratum granulosum & corneum.

It is extremely important because it acts as a semi permeable membrane
through which the infectious bacterial products pass into the gingiva &
tissue fluid from the gingiva seeps into the sulcus.
JUNCTIONAL EPITHELIUM
It consists of a collar-like band of nonkeratinised stratified squamous
epithelium.
It is 3-4 layer thick in early external life but increases with age upto 10-20 layer.
ernal It consists of 2 strata - the basal layer facing the connective tissue and the
suprabasal Payer extending to the tooth surface.
mina The junctional epithelium is formed by the confluence of the oral epithelium
and the REE during tooth eruption.
The length of the JE is 0.25-1.35 mm.
The attachment of the $ JE to the tooth is reinfarced by the gingival fibres,
which brace the marginal gingiva against the tooth surface.
For this reason the JE and the gingival fibers are considered as functional
unit, called as the dentogingival unit.
CELLS OF THE GINGIVAL EPITHELIUM
KERATINOCYTES
Principal cell type of GE which undergo proliferation & differentiation
Proliferation takes place by mitosis in the basal layer
Differentiation involves the process of keratinisation, which consists of a
sequence of biochemical & marphologic events that occur in the cell as it
migrates from basal layer
A complete keratinisation Leads to the production of arthokeratinised super-
ficial harny layer, with no nuclei in stratum corneum & well defined
stratum granulesum.
MELANOCYTES
Melanocytes are dendritic cells located in the basal and spinous layers of the
Gingival epithelium.
They synthesise melanin in organelles called premelorasones/welanasone
LANGERHANS CELLS
They are dendritic cells located among the keratinocytes at all suprabasal
level.
They are modified monocytes derived from bone marrow.
They have an important role in the immune reaction as antigen presentin
cells for lymphocytes.
They are found in oral epithelium of normal gingiva & in smaller amounts
in the sulcular epithelium, but are probably absent from JE of
normal gingiva.

MERKEL CELLS
Located in the deeper layers of epithelium
They harbor nerve endings and act as tactile perceptars
GINGIVAL FLUID / SULCULAR FLUID
It can be considered as either a transudate or exudate.
In the healthy sulcus, the amount of gingival fluid is very small, while during
inflammation, the gingival fluid flow increases, & its composition resembles
that of aninflammatory exudate.
GINGIVAL CONNECTIVE TISSUE
The connective tissue of the gingiva is called as lamina propria.
It consists of 2 Layers - a> a papillary Layer subjacent to the epithelium
b> areticular Payer contiguous with the periasteum of
alveolar bone

GINGIVAL FIBERS
The major components of gingival connective tissue are collagen fibres (60% volume)
The gingival fibers are arranged in 3 groups -
A> GINGIVODENTAL GROUP
The gingivodental fibres are those on the facial lingual and interproximal
surfaces
They are embedded in the cementum just beneath the epithelium at the base of
the gingival sulcus.
B> CIRCULAR GROUP
The circular fibres course through the connective tissue of the marginal and
interdental ging iva & excircle the tooth in a ringlike fashion.
On the facial & lingual surfaces, they project from the cementum in fax-like
conformation towards the crest and outer surface of marginal gingiva.

c> TRANS SEPTAL GROUP
Located interproximally, the trans septal fibres form horizontal bundles that
extend between the cementum of approximating teeth into which they are
embedded.
They Lie in the area between the epithelium at the base of the gingival sulcus
& the crest of the interdental bone & are sometimes classified with the
principle fibres of PDL.

DEFENSE MECHANISM OF GINGIVA
The various mechanisms that play an important role in the defence of periodontal
tissues against infection are -
1> Epithelial Barrier [Junctional Epithelium]
2> Gingival crevicular Fluid
3> Saliva
4> Leucocytes in the dentogingivalarea
1. EPITHELIAL BARRIER
The stratified squamous epithelium of gingiva plays an activerole in innate
host defense by responding to bacteria in an interactive manner.
The principal cells of the gingival epithelium are the keratinocytes, which undergo
proliseration and differentiation
Differentiation of keratinocytes produces an arthokeratinised superficial harry
layer, which forms a protective barrier.
Some other proteins are also synthesised during maturation of keratinocytes;
namely keratolinin and involucrin. These proteins form a chemically resistant
structure called as envelope below the cell membrane.
ROLE OF JUNCTIONAL EPITHELIUM
Junctional epithelium exhibits several structural and functional features that
contribute to preventing pathogenic bacterial flora from colonizing the subgingival
tooth surface.
The features are-
<a> JE is firmly attached to the surface. farming an epithelial barrier against
tooth bacteria
<b> Itallows access of gingival fluid, inflammatory cells, and components of the
immunologic host defense to the gingival margin
<c> JE cells exhibit rapid turnover, which contributes to the host-parasite
equilibrium & rapid repair of damaged tissue.
2. GINGIVAL CREVICULAR FLUID
GCF is an inflammatory exudate
Hence, the amount of GCF is greater when inflammation is present and is
sometimes propartional to the severity of inflammation.
GCF production is not increased by Trauma fromocclusion but is increased by
mastication of coarse foods, toothbrushing, gingival massage ovulation,
smoking and harmonal contraceptives.

3. SALIVA
Salivary secretions are protective in nature because they maintain the oral
tissues in a physiologic state.
Saliva maintains pH in the oral cavity and helps neutralise reflux acids in
the oesophagus.
Bicarbonates and phasphate ions as well as basic proteins in saliva maintain
the near neutral pH in the oral cavity, which prevents demineralization
of enamel that would otherwise occur due to acids produced by the sugar-
metabolising bacteria.
The fluid nature of saliva provides a washing and cleansing action that clears
non-adherent, potentially harmful substances in the oral cavity.
Viscous components eg mucin lubricate oral tissues and form barrier
against microbial products.
Salivary proteins form a thin coating on tooth surfaces called the pellicle,
that protects those surfaces.
Saliva is supersaturated with calcium and phasphate ions, which leads to
past-eruptive maturation of enamel surfaces, which increases their hardness
and resistance to dimoineratisation.
Predominant antibody in saliva is IgA. IgA inhibits the attachment of
oral Streptococcus species to epithelial cells.
ANTIBACTERIAL FACTORS
Lysozyme is a hydrolytic enzyme effective against both gram- +ve and gram
-ve organisms specifically against Veillonella & Actinobacillus species.
Lactoperoxidase-thoyanate is effective against Lactobacillus &
Streptococcus by preventing the accumulation of Lysine & glutamic acid,
both of which are essential for bacterial growth.

Another antibacterial finding is lactoferrin, effective against Actinobacillus
species.
4. LEUKOCYTES
PMNs are the most common leukocytes present in the gingival sulcus
Neutrophils are the first Line of defense in the dentogingival area
Gingival sulcus is the part of entry of leukocytes into the oral cavity.
Leukocytes are present in gingival sulcus even when histologic area are free
of inflammatory exudate infiltrate
Differential count of Leucocytes from clinically healthy human gingival sulci
have shown 91% PMNs and 8.5% mononuclear cells
Mononuclear cells have 58% B cells, 24% T cells and 18% mononuclear
phagocytes
The ratio of T cells: B cells is reversed from normal 3:1 in peripheral blood
to 1:3 in GCF

THEORIES OF CALCULUS FORMATION
Dental calculus is defined as an adherent, mineralised bacterial plaque that
forms on the surfaces of natural teeth and dental prastheses.
THEORIES OF CALCULUS FORMATION
A. Precipitation Theory
B. Epitactic concept / Heterogenous Nucleation concept
c. Inhibition Theory
A. PRECIPITATION THEORY
Mineral precipitation results from a local rise in the degree of saturation
of calcium and phasphate ions, which occurs due to following reasons-
i>
A rise in the pH of saliva causes precipitation of calcium and phasphate salts
by lowering the precipitation constant.
The pH may be elevated by loss of carbon dioxide and formation of ammonia
by dental plaque bacteria or by protein degradation during stagnation.
ii> Colloidal proteins in saliva bind calcium and phasphate ions and maintain
a supersaturated solution with respect to calcium phasphate salts.
With stagnation of saliva, colloids settle out and the supersaturated state
is no longer maintained , leading to precipitation of calcium phasphate satts.
iii> Phasphatase Liberated from plaque, desquamated
epithelial cells and bacteria
Hydrolyses organic phasphates in saliva
Increases concentration of free phasphate ions
Precipitates calcium phasphate
iv> Esterases present in dental plaque, bacteria,
macrophages and desquamated epithelial cells
Hyrolyses d fatty esters into free fatty acids
Fatty acids form soap with calcium and magnesium
Converts to less soluble calcium phasphate salts

B. EPITACTIC CONCEPT
According to this concept, calculus formation may be initiated through epitaxis
by organic complexes in the matrix.
Epitaxis means crystal formation of a compound through seeding. The formation
of the initial crystal is called nucleation.
The intercellular matrix provides the architectural template for the initial
hydroxyapatite crystal.
This focus of calcification enlarges and coalesces to form a calcified mass.
Seeding or nucleating agents in calculus are not clearly known. They are-
suspected to be the intercellular matrix of plaque, plaque bacteria.
Thecarbohydrate-protein complexes may initiate calcification by removing
calcium from the saliva (chelation) and binding with it to form nuclei
that induce subsequent deposition of minerals.
C. INHIBITION THEORY
According to this theory, the possibility of calcified mass occuring only at
specific sites is because of existence of an inhibiting mechanism at non-
calcifying site.
When calcification occurs, the inhibitors are apparantly removed/altered.
Pyrophasphate is one possible inhibiting substance thought to inhibit
calcification by preventing the initial nucleus from growing by poisoning
the growth centerof the crystal.

PERIODONTAL LIGAMENT
The periodontal Ligament is a complex vascular and highly cellular connective
tissue that surrounds the tooth root and connects it to the inner wall of the
alveolar bone.
COMPOSITION
1> Periodontal fibres
principal fibres
2> Cellular elements
secondary fibres
3> Ground substance
PRINCIPAL FIBRES OF PDL
The most important elements of the PDL are the principal fibres.
These are collagenous and arranged in bundles and follow a wavy course
when viewed in longitudinal section.
The terminal partions of the principal fibres that are inserted into cementum
and bone are termed as Sharpey's fibres.
Once embedded in the wall of the alveolus or in the teeth, Sharpey's fibres
calcify to a significant degree.
They are associated with abundant non-collagenous proteins typically found
in bone and tooth comentum. They we most notable among them are
asteopontin and bone sialoprotein.
The principal fibres are arranged in 6 groups that develop sequentially in
the developing root. They are - alstra
- Transseptal
- Alveolar crest
- Horizontal group
- Oblique group
- Apical group
I Interradicular group
i> TRANSSEPTAL GROUP
- Transseptal fibres extend interproximally over the alveolar bone crest &
are embedded in the cementum of adjacent teeth.
- They are reconstructed even after destruction of the alveolar bone resulting
from periodontal disease.
- These fibres may be considered as belonging to the gingiva because they do.
not have asseons attachment.

ii>. ALVEOLAR CREST GROUP
ТИЗМАДА
70
- Alveolar crest fibers extend obliquely from the cementum just beneath
the junctional epithelium to the alveolar crest.
- Fibres also run from the cementum over the alveolar crest and to the
fibrous Layer of the periosteum covering alveolar bone.
- The alveolar crest fibers prevent the extrusion of the tooth and resist
Lateral tooth movements.
- The incision of these fibers during periodontal surgery does not increase
tooth mobility unless significant attachment lass has occured.
iii> HORIZONTAL GROUP
- Harizontal fibers extend at right angles to the long axis of the tooth from
the cementum to the alveolar bone.
iv> APICAL GROUP
- Apical fibres radiate in a rather irregular manner from the cementum
to the bone at the apical region of the socket.
- They donot occur on incompletely formed roots.
v> OBLIQUE GROUP
- Oblique fibers, the largest group in the PDL, extend from the cementum in
a caronal direction obliquely to the bone.
- They bear the brunt of vertical masticatory stresses and transform
them into tension on the alveolar bone.
vi> INTERRADICULAR GROUP
- The interradicular fibres fanout from the cementum to the tooth bone in the
furcation areas of multirooted teeth.

FUNCTIONS OF PERIODONTAL LIGAMENT
The functions of the periodontal ligament are categorised into
A. Physical
B. Farmative & Remodelling
C. Nutritional & Sensory
A. PHYSICAL FUNCTIONS
- Provision of a soft tissue casing to protect the vessels and nerves from injury
by mechanical forces
- Transmission of occlusal forces to the bone
- Attachment of the teeth to the bone
PTA PTAMR MR
- Maintenance of the gingival tissues in their proper relationship to the teeth
- Resistance to the impact of occlusal forces [shock absorption]
B. FORMATIVE & REMODELLING FUNCTION
- PDL and alveolar bone cells are exposed to physical forces in response to
mastication, parafunction, speech and arthodontic tooth movement.
- Cells of the PDL participate in the formation and resorption of cernentum &
bone, which occur in physiological tooth movement, in the accomodation of
the periodontium to occlusal forces and in the repair of injuries.
- The PDL is constantly undergoing remodelling. Old cells and fibres are
broken down and replaced by new ones and mitotic activity can be observed
in the fibroblasts and endothelial cells.
- Fibroblasts form the collagen fibres, and the residual filres mesenchymal
cells develop into asteoblasts and cementoblasts.
- Therefore, the rate of formation, and the differentiation of asteoblasts, cemento-
blasts and fibroblasts affect the rate of formation of collagen, bone cementum and
bone. collagen
C. NUTRITIONAL & SENSORY FUNCTION
- The PDL supplies nutrients to the cementum, bone andgingiva by way of the
Blood vessels and also provide Lymphatic drainage.
- Inrelationto other ligaments and tendons, the PDL is a highly vascularised
tissue. This relatively high blood vessel content may provide hydrodyna-
mic damping to applied forces as well as high perfusion rates of the PDL.
- The PDL is abundantly supplied with sensory nerve findings fibres
capable of transmitting tactile, pressure and pain sensations by the
trigeminal pathways. Nerve bundles pass into the PDL from the periapical
area and through channels from the alveolar bone that follow the
course of the blood vessels.

- The bundles divide into single myelinated fibres, which ultimately Pase their
myelin sheaths and end in one of the 4 types of neural transmission
a> Free endings which have atree-like transmission configuration and
carry pain sensation
b> Ruffini-like mechanoreceptors - -located primarily in the periapical area
c> Coiled Meissner's corpuscles - mechanoreceptors, found in the midroot
d> Spindle Like pressure and vibration endings
region
- surrounded by afibrous capsule and located mainly in the apex.

PERIODONTAL POCKET
Periodontal pocket is defined as as pathologically deepened gingival sulcus,
and is one of the most important clinical features of the periodontal disease.
CLASSIFICATION
1>
Pocket
Gingival pocket
Periodontal pocket [True Pocket]
[False pocket]
Suprabony
Infrabony
Gingival pocket - formed by gingival enlargement without destruction of
the underlying periodontal tissue
Periodontal pocket - produces destruction of the supporting periodontal
tissues, leading to loasening and exfoliation of teeth.
Suprabony / Supraalveolar pocket - in which the bottom of the pocket
is caronal to the underlying alveolar bone.
Infrabony / Intraalveolar - in which the bottom of the pocket is apical
to the level of the adjacent alveolar bone.
2> Based on the number of tooth surfaces involved
Simple - involves only one tooth surface
Compound - two or ml more tooth surfaces are involved
Complex - it is a spiral pocket that originates on one tooth surface
and twists around the teeth to involve one or more addition-
-al surfaces. [SPIRAL POCKET]
CONTENTS OF PERIODONTAL POCKET
The periodontal pocket consists of debris, principally containing microbes &
their products like enzymes, endotoxins and other metabolic products,
dental plaque, gingival fluid, food remnants, salivary mucin, desquam
- -ated epithelial cells and leukocytes.
If purulent exudate is present, it consists of living, degenerated and
necrotic leukocytes (PMNL), Living and dead bacteria, serum and a
scanty amount a of fibrin.
Pus formation is a common feature in periodontal disease but it is only
a secondary sign.

PATHOGENESIS OF PERIODONTAL POCKET
The initial lesion in the development of periodontitis is the inflammation of the
gingiva in response to a bacterial challenge.
Changes involved in the transition from the normal gingival sulcus to the
pathologic periodontal pocket are associated with different proportions
of bacterial cells in dental plaque.
Pocket formation start as an inflammatory change
in connective tissue wall of gingival sulcus
Inflammatory exudates cause degeneration of surrounding
connective tissue, including gingival fibres
Just apical to JE, collagen fibres are destroyed and area

CHRONIC PERIODONTITIS
170
PERIODONTITIS - Periodontitis is defined as an inflammatory disease of
the supporting tissues of the teeth caused by specific microorganisms
or groups of specific organisms microorganisms, resulting in progress ive
destruction of periodontal ligament and alveolar bone, with increased
probing depth formation, recession, or both.
CHRONIC PERIODONTITIS - Chronic periodontitis has beendefinedas an
infectious disease resulting in inflammation within the supporting tissues
of the teeth, progressive attachment Loss, and bone lass.
CLINICAL FEATURES
A. SIGNS
- Supra-and subgingival plaque accumulation
- calculus formation
- Gingival inflamation
- Lass of attachment and alveolar bone loss
- Pocket formation
- Occassional suppuration
- Poor oral hygiene
- Lass of gingival stippling
- Gingiva appears slightly to moderately swollen, pale red to magenta-
- Bleeding spontaneously or in response to probing
B. SYMPTOMS
- Bleeding gums enbrushing or eating
- Spaces occur between the teeth as a result of tooth movement
- Usually painless, pain occurs occasionally duet to exposed roots that
are sensitized to heat, cold or both.
- Areas of localised dull pain, sometimes radiating deep within the jaw.
- Presence of areas of food impaction may & add to patients discomfort.
- Gingival tenderness or itchiness
RISK FACTORS FOR CHRONIC PERIODONTITIS
A. LOCAL FACTORS
- In periodontal destruction, plaque accumulation is the primary
initiating factor
- In addition to this, plaque retentive factors are also important in the
development and progression of chronic periodontitis.

i. Plaque accumulation
- Plaque that accumulates on the tooth and gingival surfaces at the DEJ
is considered to be the main actiologic factor in periodontal destruction
- Parphyromonas gingivalis Tannerella farsythia and Trepasonema
denticola are associated with okgoing attachment and bone loss in
chronic periodontitis.
ii. Plaque Retention
- Those factors which facilitate plaque accumulation or prevent the
removal of plaque by routine oral hygiene procedures are called
plaque retentive factors.
- Calculus is considered the most important plaque retentive factor
because its ability to retain and harbar plaque bacteria on its rough
surface.
- Other factors that are known to retain plaque are subgingival and
overhanging margins of restarations, carious lesions that extend
subgingivally, furcations exposed by loss of bone, crowded and
malaligned teeth.
B. SYSTEMIC FACTORS
- The rate of progression in plaque-induced chronic periodontitis is considered
to be slow. But when it occurs in apatient who has a systemic disease that
influences the effectiveness of hast response, the rate of periodontal
destruction is significantly increased.
- Non insulin dependent Diabetes Mellitus [NIDDM] is B one of the most
significant systemic disease that aggravates periodontal destruction
c. ENVIRONMENTAL & BEHAVIORAL FACTORS
- Smoking is one factor that has been found to increase the severity of perio-
dontal disease.
1 It is seen that smokers with chronic periodontitis have more attachment
and bone less lass, more furcation involvements and deeper pockets
compared with non-smokers.
- Emotional stress can also influence the extent of chronic periodontitis
9. GENETIC FACTORS
- Periodontal destruction frequently seen among family members and
across different generations suggest the passibility that there is an
underlying genetic factor that plays a role in periodontal disease.

TREATMENT
A. NON SURGICAL
Initial therapy [scaling and root planing]
Antimicrobial therapy - as an adjunct to routine periodontal therapy
Improv ement in oral hygiene
Instruction, reinforcement, evaluation of plaque control records
Removal of all the factors contributing to plaque accumulation eq-
correction of ill-fitting dentures, overeontoured crowns, overhanging
restarations etc.
B. SURGICAL THERAPY
Pocket elimination procedures
Regenerative therapy
- Box Bone replacement grafts'
- Guided tissue regeneration
- Combined regenerative techniques
Resective therapy
- Flaps with or without asseous surgery
- Gingivectomy

APICALLY DISPLACED FLAP
The apically displaced flap technique can be used for
a> pocket eradication
b> widening of the zone of attached gingiva
Depending on the purpose, it can be a full-thickness (mucoperiasteal) flap
or a split-thickness (mucasal) flap.
The split-thickness flap requires more precision and time, as well as a
/
gingival tissue thick enough to split, but it canbe more accurately positioned
and sutured in an apical position using a periasteal suturing technique.
SURGICAL PROCEDURE
Step 1 - An internal bevel incision is made.
To preserve as much as keratinised and attached gingiva as passible,
it should be no more than about 1mm from the crest of the gingiva
and directed to the crest of the bone.
The incision is made after the existing scalloping ,and there is no need
to mark the bottom of the pocket in the external gingival surface.
because the incision is unrelated to pocket depth.
It is also not necessary to accentuate the scallop interdentally because
the flap is displaced apically not placed interdentally.
Step 2 - Crevicular incisions are made, followed by initial elevation of the flap
then interdental incisions are performed and the wedge of the tissue
that contains the pocket wall is removed.
Step 3 - Vertical incisions are made extending beyond the mucogingival junction
If the objective is a full thickness flap, it is elevated by blunt dissection
using a periasteal elevator.
If a split-thickness flap is required, it is elevated using sharp
dissection with a Bard-Parker knife to split it, leaving a Payer
of connective tissue, including the periosteum, on the bone.
Step 4- After removal of all granulation tissue, scaling and root planing,
and asseous surgery if needed, the flap is displaced apically.
It is important that the vertical incisions and therefore the flap
elevation, reach past the mucogingival junction to provide
adequate mobility to the flap for its apical displacement.
Step 5 - If a full-thickness flap was performed a sling suture around the
tooth prevents the flap from sliding to a position more apical than
that desired, and the periodontal dressings can avoid its movement
in a caronal direction.

A partial thickness flap is sutured to the periosteum using a direct Loop suture
or a combination of Loop and anchor suture.
A dry foil is placed over the flap before covering it with the dressing to
prevent the introduction of pack under the flap.
After 1 week dressings and sutures are removed. The area is usually
repacked for another week, after which the patient is instructed to use
chlorhexidine mouth rinse or to apply chlorhexidine topically with cotton-
tipped applicators for another 2-3 weeks.

PAPILLA. PRESERVATION FLAP
Greater crown and root exposure and increase in interdental spacing
following flap surgery is highly unacceptable.
An ideal periodontal therapy must necessarily consider esthetic
appearance, which means an effort to maintain gingival marginal
anatomy and as much height of papilla as passible along the course
of the periodontal therapy.
A surgical approach that splits the papilla certainly contribute to
shrinkage and decrease in the height of interdental papilla leading to
exposure of the interproximal embrasures.
This led to the development of papilla preservation flap.
[Takei etal]
SURGICAL PROCEDURE
Step 1- A crevicular incision is made around each tooth with no incisions
across interdental papilla
Step 2- The preserved papilla canbe incorporated into the facial or lingual/
palatal flap, although it is most often integrated into the facial flap.
In these cases, the lingual or palatal incision consists of a semilunar
incision across the interdental papilla in its palatal or lingual aspect
Step 3 - An arbanknife is then introduced into this incision to sever half to 2/3
the base of interdental papilla.
The papilla is then dissected from the lingual or palatal aspect and
elevated intact with the facial flap.
Step 4- The flap is reflected without thinning the tissue.

DENTAL PLAQUE
gental plaque is defined as a structured, resilient, yellow-grayish substance
that adheres tenaciously to the intra oral hard substances, including fix
removable and fixed restarations.
It is of 2 types mainly
a> Supragingival plaque - at or above the gingival margin
b> Subgingival plaque - found below the gingival margin, between the
tooth and gingival pocket epithelium
PROCESS OF FORMATION OF PLAQUE
The process of plaque formation can be divided into 3 phases
A. Farmation of the pellicle on the tooth surface
B. Initial adhesion and attachment of bacteria
C. Colonization and plaque maturation
A. FORMATION OF PELLICLE
- All surfaces in the oral cavity including hard and soft tissues, are coated
with a layer of organic material knownas acquired pellicle.
-
The pellicle consists of variety of proteins, glycoproteins & peptides & other
molecules that can function as adhesion sites (receptars) for bacteria.
Pellide is composed of 2 layers - a thin basal layer that is very difficult
to remove even with harsh chemical and mechanical treatments, and a
thicker globular Layer (upto 1 um), that is easier to detach.
Thus, dental enamel is permanently covered with an acquired pellicle from
the moment that teeth erupt.
- Consequently, bacteria that adheres to tooth surfaces do not contact the
enamel directly but interact with the acquired pellicle.
- Many proteins retain enzymatic activity when incorporated into the pellicle,
and some of them may affect the physiology of adhering bacterial cells.
B. INITIAL ADHESION OF BACTERIA
Formation of pellicle aids in adhesion of some bacteria on the tooth surface.
Receptors They are called as primary colonisers.
- It occurs due to specific interaction between microbial cell surface
adhesin molecules and the receptars in the pellicle.
The prominent primary colonisers are Streptococcus species [60-80%
over the first 4-8 hours.
-
Other organisms present are Haemophilus, Nisseria sp. and Actenomyces
so.
- These primary colonisers provide new binding sites for adhesion by
other oral bacteria.

- The metabolic activity of the primary colonisers modifies the Local
microenvironment in ways that can influence the ability of other
bacteria to survive in the dental plaque.
- The attachment & adhesion of bacteria is studied in 3 phases
Phase 1 - Transpart to the surface
it involves initial transport of bacterium to the tooth surface.
Since relatively few oral bacteria are motile; forces such as
saliva flow and mechanical contact are very important for
initial transport.
Phase 2- Initial Adhesion
It involves an initial, reversible adhesion of the bacterium [initiated
by interaction between the bacterium and the surface, from a
certain distance] through Vander Waals attractive forces and electro-
static repulsive forces.
Phase 3- Strong Attachment
After initial adhesion, a firm ancharage between the bacterium &
surface is established.
Ona rough surface, bacteria are better protected against shear
forces so that a change from reversible to Irreversible binding
may occur more easily and more frequently.
C. COLONIZATION & PLAQUE MATURATION
- The initial colonisers make use of the available O2, leading to reduced
Oxygen Levels favouring the growth of gramnegative anaerobic
organisms such as Fusobacterium nucleatum and Capnocytophaga
species.
- These organisms adhere to cell surface receptars of the initial colonizers
as they have poor ability to get attached directly to the pellicle, and
hence as the plaque matures there is considerable increase in the number
of gram negative bacteria.
- Bacterial adherence to one another is termed as coagaregation and it plays
an important role in the process of secondary colonisation.
- In the early stages of plaque formation, there is coaggregation between
gram negative and gram positive arganisms such as F. nucleatum
and A. viscasus followed by coaggregation in the later stages between
gram negative organisms eg nucleatum and P. gingivalis.

- Nutrients are required for growth of the bacterial colonies. The main source
of nutrition in supragingival plaque is saliva.
- In established plaque substances produced by certain species become
nutrients that are essential for the growth of other organisms.
examples - Lactate and formate produced by streptococci and Actinomyces
species are used by Veinol Veillonella as an energy source.

When occlusal forces exceeds the adaptive capacity of tissues tissue injury
results which is called trauma from occlusion. [GLickman]
It is of 2 types - a> Primary trauma from occlusion
b> Secondary trauma from occlusion
When trauma from occlusion is the result of alterations in occusal forces
it is called primary occlusal trauma.
When TFO results from reduced capacity of the periodontium to withstand
occlusal forces it is known as secondary trauma from occlusion.
Caused by reduced adaptive capacity duet to
0> bone and attachment loss
b> systemic diseases can also affect adaptive capacity of supportive tissues
This reduces the periodontal attachment area and alters the leverage grip of the
remaining tissues. The periodontium becomes more vulnerable to injury,
and previously well-tolerated occlusal forces become traumatic.

TISSUE RESPONSE TO INCREASED
OCCLUSAL FORCES
Tissue response to increased ocelusal forces occur in three stages -
injury, repair and adaptive remodelling of the periodontium
STAGE - INJURY
Tissue injury is produced by excessive occlusal forces. The body then attempts
to repair the injury and restore the periodontium.
This can occur if the forces are diminished or if the tooth drifts' away from them.
The areas of the periodontium most susceptible to injury from excessive
occlusal force are the furcations.
Injury to the periodontium produces a temporary depression in mitotic
activity and rate of proliferation and differentiation of fibroblasts, in
collagen formation and in bone formation.
They return to normal Levels after dissipation of forces.
Under the forces of occlusion, a tooth rotates around a fulcrum or axis of
rotation. This creates areas of pressure and tension on opposite sides
of the fulcrum.
Slight excessive pressure stimulates
-resarption of alveolar bone
- widening of PDL space
Slight excessive tension causes
- apposition of alveolar bone
- elongation of PDL fibres
Greater pressure produces
compression of the fibres
which produces
areas of hyalinisation. Subsequent injury to the fibroblasts and other &
connective tissue cells leads to necrosis of areas of PDL.
Severe tension causes widening of PDL, thrombasis, hemorrhage,
tearing of PDL and resarption of alveolar bone.
STAGE 2-REPAIR
Repair is constantly occuring in the normal periodontium, and trauma
from occlusion stimulates increased reparative activity.
The damaged tissues are removed, and new connective tissue cells and
fibers, bone and cementum are formed in an attempt to restare the
injured periodontum.
Farces remain traumatic only as long as the damage produced exceeds
the reparative capacity of the tissues.

When bone is resorbed by excessive occlusal forces the body attempts to
reinfarce the thinned bony trabecular with new bone. This attempt to
compensate for last bone is called buttressing Bone formation and is an
important feature of the reparative process associated with TFO.
Buttressing bone formation occurs within the jaws [central buttressing]
and on the bone surface [peripheral buttressing
STAGE 3- ADAPTIVE REMODELLING
If the repair process can not keep pace with the destruction caused by the
occlusion, the periodontium is remodelled in an effort to create a
structural relationship in which the forces are no Longer injurious to the
tissues.
This results in a widened periodontal ligament, whichis funnel ishaped at
the crest, and angular defects in the bone, with no pocket formation.
The involved teeth become loase. Increased vascularisation is noticed.
The injury phase shows an increase in the areas of resarption and decrease
in bone formation, whereas the repair phase demonstrates decreased
resorption and increased bone formation after adaptive remod
After adaptive remodelling, resarption and formation return to normal.

HISTOPATHOLOGY OF
PERIODONTAL POCKET
The connective tissue is oedematous and densely infiltrated with plasma
cells and Lymphocytes and scattered PMNs.
Blood vessels are increased in number, dilated and engarged, single or
multiple necrotic foci are occasionally present
Connective tissue also shows proliferation of the endothelial cells with
newly formed capillaries, fibroblasts and collagen fibres.
The junctional epithelium at the base of the pocket is usually much
shorter than that of a normal sulcus.
The carono-apical length of the JE is 50-100 u.
The most severe degenerative changes in the periodontal pocket occur
along the lateral wall.
Epithelial buds or interlacing cards of epithelial cells project from the
Pateral wall into the adjacent inflamed connective tissue and frequently
extend further apically than the JE.
Progressive degeneration and necrasis of the epithelium leads to ulceration
of the lateral wall, exposure of underlying marked inflamed connective-
tissue and suppuration.
The severity of the degenerative changes is not necessarily related to
pocket depth. Ulceration of the lateral wall may occur in shallow pockets
and deep pockets are occasionally observed in which the lateral epithel-
ium is relatively intact and shows slight degeneration.
The epithelium at the gingival crest of a periodontal pocket is generally
intact and thickened with prominent rete pegs.
Filaments, rods and coccoid organisms with predominant Gram negative
cell walls have been found in the intercellular spaces initially of the
epithelium.
Some bacteria traverse the basement lamina and invade the subepithelial
connective tissue.

PROGNOSIS
The prognasis is a prediction of the probable course, duration and outcome
of a disease based on a general knowledge of the pathogenesis of the disease
and the presence of risk factors for the disease.
It is established after the diagnosis is made and before the treatment
plan is established.
TYPES OF PROGNOSIS
A> BASED ON STUDIES EVALUATING TOOTH MORTALITY
good prognasis - control of etiologic factors and adequate periodontal
support ensure the tooth will be easy to maintain by
the patient and clinician
Fair prognasis - approximately 25% attachment Loss and/or class I
furcation involvement [Location and depthallow
proper maintenance with good patient compliance]
Poor prognasis - 50% attachment loss, class II furcation involvement
[location and depth make maintenance passible but
difficult ]
Questionable prognasis - > 50% attachment Lass, poor crownto root
ratio, poor root form, class II furcations [location &
depth makes access difficult] or class III furcation invol-
vement, > 2t mobility, root proximity
Hopeless prognasis - Inadequate attachment to maintain health comfort
and function
Note :- Good, fair and hopeless prognases in this classification system
could be established with a reasonable degree of accuracy.
However, poor and questionable progranases were Likely to
change to other categories because they depend on a Large no.
of factors that can interact in an unpredictable no of ways.

B> BASED ON PROBABILITY OF OBTAINING STABILITY
Favorable prognasis - comprehensive periodontal treatment and maintenance
will stabilise the status of the tooth.
Future lass of periodontal support is unlikely.
Questionable prognasis - Local and systemic factors influencing the periodon-
tal status of the tooth may or may not be controllable.
If controlled, the periodontal status canbe stabilised
with comprehensive periodontal treatment.
If not, future periodontal breakdown may occur.
Unfavarable prognasis - Local and systemicfactor influencing the periodon-
tal status cannot be controlled.
Comprehensive periodontal treatment and maintenance
are unlikely to prevent future periodontal breakdown.
Hopeless prognasis - The tooth must be extracted.

When compared with older patient ,the reparative process in a younger
individual is more, but the amount of bone lost in a span of few years

2> Disease severity - Patients with a history of previous periodontal disease
may be indicative of their susceptibility for future periodontal break-
Prognasis is affected by - pocket depth

3> Plaque control - Active removal of plaque by the patient on a daily basis
is critical to the success and prognosis of periodontal therapy.
4> Patient compliance - The prognasis of for patients with gingival and
periodontal involvement is critically dependent on the patients attitude,

desire to retain the natural teeth and ability to maintain oral hygiene.
SYSTEMIC & ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
1> Smoking - Prognasis in patients who smoke and have slight to moderate
periodontitis is generally fair to poor.
In patients with sever periodontitis, the prognasis may be poor to hopeless.
2> Systemic diseases - Patients systemic background affects overall prognasis
in several ways.
Far instance, in AM, the prognasis is questionable when surgical treatment is
required.
3> Stress - stress either physical or emotional may alter the patient's ability to
respond to the periodontal treatment performed.
LOCAL FACTORS
1> Plaque/calculus - Removal of plaque/calculus is essential for having a good
prognasis
2> Subgingival restorations - Tooth with a discrepancy in its subgingival margins
has a poorer prognasis than a tooth with well contoured supragingival margin
It is because subgingival margins contribute to increased plaque accumulation,
increased inflammation and increased bone loss.
ANATOMIC FACTORS
1> Prognosis is poor with for teeth with short, tapered roots and relatively large
crowns.
2> Other anatomical considerations that present accessibility problems are
developmental grooves, root proximity and furcation involvement.
The presence of any of these can warsen the prognasis.
PROSTHETIC & RESTORATIVE FACTORS
1> Abutment Selection - The overall prognasis generally considers bone
levels and attachment Levels to establish whether the remaining teeth
serve as abutments for prasthesis.
2> Caries, Nonvital teeth and root resorption - Before undertaking
periodontal treatment for grassly decayed teeth, adequate restarative
and endodontic therapy should be considered.

RESECTIVE OSSEOUS SURGERY
REPAIR & REGENERATION
REGENERATION
It is healing by proliferation of parenchymal.cells results in complete
restoration of the original tissue architecture.
REPAIR
It is healing by proliferation of connective tissue elements resulting in
fibrasis and scarring.
Regeneration of the periodontium is a continuous physiologic process.
Under normal conditions, new cells and tissues are constantly being formed
to replace those that' mature and die, this is termed wear and tear repair.
Regeneration is occuring even during destructive periodontal disease.
Repair simply restares the continuity of the diseased marginal gingiva and
reestablishes a normal gingival sulcus at the same level on the root at the
base of the preexisting periodontal pocket.
as
Repair, also called as healing by scar, arrests bone destruction but does not
results in gain in gingival attachment or bone height.
OSSEOUS SURGERY
Osseous surgery may be defined as the procedure by which changes in the
alveolar bone can be accomplished to rid it of deformities induced by the
periodontal disease process or other related factors such as exastasis and
tooth supracruption.
It is of 2 types-<a> Additive
<b> Subtractive
A> ADDITIVE (RECONSTRUCTIVE)
Restaring the alveolar bone to its original level
B> SUBTRACTIVE (RESECTIVE)
Restaring the form of the pre-existing alveolar bone to the level present
at the time of surgery or slightly apical to this level.

RATIONALE FOR RESECTIVE OSSEOUS SURGERY
To reshape the marginal bone to resemble that of the alveolar process
undamaged by periodontal disease
To enhance the patients ability to remove plaque and oral debris
To achieve more effective maintenance therapy and greater longitudinal
stability
STEPS OF RESECTIVE OSSEOUS SURGERY
A full thickness mucoperiasteal flap should be used whenever asseous
resective surgery is contemplated.
Instruments -
a> Ostectomy - hand instruments
[Rongeur, Oshsenbein chisel, back-action chisel]
b> Osteoplasty - rotary instruments
[carbide round bar, diamond bur]
Sequences / Steps -
a> Vertical grooving
Osteoplasty
b> Radicular blending
c> Flattening interproximal bone
Ostectomy
d> Gradualizing marginal bone
A> VERTICAL GROOVING
It is the first step because it can define the general thickness and
subsequent form of alveolar housing.
It is usually done by rotary instruments
It is designed to -
- reduce the thickness of alveolar housing
- provide relative prominence to the radicular aspect of the teeth
- provide continuity from the interproximal surface on to the
radicular surface.
Indications - thick, bony margins, shallow crater formations
contra-indications - close roots or thin alveolar housing

B> RADICULAR BLENDING
It is an extension of vertical grooving.
It is an attempt to gradualize the bone over the entire radicular surface
to provide the best result for vertical grooving.
It provides smooth, blended surface for good flap adaptation.
Indication- - Thick ledge of bone on radicular surface
Contraindication - When vertical grooving is minor or radicular bone
is thin or fenestrated
Both vertical grooving and radicular blending are purely asteoplastic
techniques that do not remove the tooth supporting bone.
Classically, shallow crater formations, thick asseous Ledges of bone on the
radicular surfaces and class I and early class II furcation involvements
are treated almost entirely with these 2 steps.
c> FLATTENING INTERPROXIMAL BONE
Requires removal of very small amount of supporting bone
Indications - 1-walled interproximal defect [hemisectal defect]
Contra-indications -advanced hemiseptal defect I compromised
asseous architecture]
D> GRADUALIZING MARGINAL BONE
Minimal bone removal to provide a sound, regular base for gingival
tissue to follow
Hand instruments are favorable.
Failure to remove small bony discrepancies on the gingival Line angles
[widow's peaks] allows the tissue to rise to a higher Level than the
base of the bone Lass in the interdental area.
FLAP PLACEMENT AND CLOSURE
After resection, the flaps are positioned and sutured
Flaps may be replaced to their original position to cover the new bony
margin, or they may be apically pasitioned
Positioning the flap apically to expose the marginal bone is one method
of altering the gingiva. width of the gingiva. [denudation]
The sutures should be placed with minimal tension.

POST OPERATIVE MAINTENANCE
A second past operative visit is often performed made at the secondar
third week, and surgical site is slightly debrided.
It takes 14 to 21 days for the attachment of the flap to the underlying
bone.
Wait 6 weeks after the surgery for dental restorations.

PERIODONITAL PACKS
Also called as periodontal dressing it is a physical barrier that is placed
in the surgical site to protect the healing tissues from the forces produced
during mastication, for comfort and close adaptation.
These dressings are applied around the necks of the teeth and adjacent
tissue to cover and protect the surgical wound after periodontal surgery.
USES OF PERIODONTAL PACKS
It minimises the likelihood of pastoperative infection and haemorrhage
Facilitates healing by preventing surface trauma during mastication
Protects against pain induced by the contact of the wound with food or
with tongue during mastication.
TYPES OF PACKS
A> Zinc oxide eugenol packs
B> Non eugenol packs
ZOE PACKS
Supplied in liquid and powder form that are mixed prior to use.
These packs are based on the reaction of zinc oxide and eugenol.
Apart from Zno & eugenol, it consists of -
a> Zinc acetate as an accelerator
b> Asbestas used as binder and filler
c> Tannic acid
The addition of accelerators such as zinc acetate gives the dressing a better
working time.
In some patients, engenol may produce allergic reactions that may render
the area erythematous combined with a burning sensation.
NON EUGENOL PACKS [COE PACK]
It is based on the reaction between a metallic oxide and fatty acids.
Supplied in 2 tubes, the contents of which are mixed immediately
before use until a uniform color is obtained.
One tube contains zinc oxide, oil for plasticity, a gum for cohesiveness
and lorothidol [a fungicide].
The other tube contains liquid coconut fatty acids thickened with
colophony rasin and chlorothymol [a bacteriastatic agent]

Other non eugenol packs include barric acid, cyanoacrylates and tissue
conditioners [methacrylate gels]
It has better fracture strength than eugenol packs.
RETENTION OF PACKS
Retention is obtained by mechanical interlocking of the interdental spaces
and by joining lingual and facial portions of the pack.
Retention of the pack may be difficult in isolated teeth or when several
teeth in the arch are missing. Numerous reinfarcements splints and
stents are required forthis.
PREPARATION AND APPLICATION

paste is formed

containing the accelerator and the base until the resulting paste is of
uniform colour

7. The dressing should cover the gingiva but overextension into uninvolved

They have been frequently used in treating refractory periodontitis,
including localised aggressive periodontitis.
Tetracyclines have the ability to concentrate in the periodontal tissues
and inhibit the growth of Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitars.
In addition, they exert an anticollagenase effect that can inhibit tissue
destruction and may aid bone regeneration.
PHARMACOLOGY
-biotics
The tetracyclines are a group of antibodies produced naturally from certain
species of Streptomyces or derived semisynthetically.
These antibiotics are bacteriastatic and are effective against rapidly multiplying
bacteria. They generally are more effective against gram tve bacteria.
They are effective in treating periodontal diseases in part because their
concentration in the gingival crevice is 2-10 Times that in serum.
CLINICAL USES
Tetracyclines have been investigatedas adjuncts in the treatment of LAP.
Systemic tetracycline can eliminate tissue bacteria and has been shown
to arrest bone loss and suppress A. actinomycetemcomitans levels in
conjunction with scaling and root planing.
This combination allows mechanical removal of root surface deposits &
elimination of pathogenic bacteria from within the tissues.
But it is not at advisable to prescribe long-term regimens of tetracyclines
because of development of resistant bacterial strains.
Although used in the past for LAP and other types of aggressive perio-
dontitis, tetracyclines no are now replaced by more effective antibiotics.
SPECIFIC AGENTS
Tetracycline, minocycline and doxycycline are semisynthetic members
of tetracycline group that have been used in periodontal therapy.

PERICORONITIS
Pericaronitis refers to the inflammation of the gingiva or a pericaronal
flap in relation to crown of an incompletely erupted teeth.
It occurs most often in the mandibular third molar area.
Pericoronitis may be acute, subacute or chronic.
CLINICAL FEATURES
The space between the crown of the teeth and overlying gingival flap [operculum]
is an ideal area for the accumulation of food debris and bacterial growth.
Even in patients with no clinical signs or symptoms, the gingival flap is
often chronically inflamed and injected and has varying degrees of ulceration
along its inner surface.
The inflammation may be exacerbated by trauma, occlusion or a foreign
body trapped underneath the tissue flap.
The inflammatory exudate increase the bulk of the flap, which may interfere
with complete clasure of the jaws and can be traumatised by contact with the
opposing jaws.
The resulting manifestations are -
a> swollen, red, a tender, suppurating lesion
b> radiating pain to the ear, throat and floor of mouth.
c> swelling of cheek region near the angle of jaw
d> lymphadenitis
e> trismus, foul taste
f> systemic complications such as fever, leucocytasis, and malaise
TREATMENT
The management of pericaronitis depends on-
a> severity of inflammation
b> the systemic complications
c> passibility of retaining of tooth
The treatment of acute pericoronitis consists of -
a) gently flushing the area with warm water to remove debris and
exudates
b> swabbing with antiseptics after elevating the flap gently from
the tooth with a scaler.
c> occlusion is evaluated to determine if an opposing tooth is
occluding with the pericaronal flap. Occlusal adjustment
is necessary in case of oppasing tooth traumatising the flap.
d) B Antibiotics in severe cases and far patients with diffuse
microbial infiltration of the tissue.

e> If the gingival flap is swollen and fluctuant, an incision may be necessary to
establish drainage and relieve pain and pressure.
After resolution of the acute symptoms, the prognasis of tooth can be evaluated.
i.e whether there is possibility of erupting to a functional position or if the factors
predispasing to pericaronitis will persist.
Bone loss on the distal surface of the second molar is a concern when third
molars are impacted along the distal surface.
The problem is significantly greater if the 3rd molars are extracted after the
roots are formed or when patients are older.
-
Thus, to reduce the risk of bone Loss around 2nd molars, partially or completely
completely impacted 3rd molars should be extracted early in their development
If the involved tooth is to be retained, the pericoronal flap is surgically removed.
It is necessary to remove both the operculum [flap] and the tissue distal
to the teeth.
Incising only the occlusal partion of the flap leaves a deep distal pocket,
which leads to recurrence of acute pericaronal involvement.

CURETTES
The curette is the instrument of choice for removing deep subgingival calculus,
coot planing altered cementum, and removing the soft tissue lining the periodontal
pocket
The curette is finer than the sickle scalers and does not have any sharp points or
corners other than the cutting edges of the blade.
The curved blade and rounded tip [toe] of the curette allow the blade to adapt
better to the root surface, unli thus avoiding tissue laceration and trauma.
In crass section the blade appears semicircular with a convexbase.
A> UNIVERSAL CURETTE
These curettes have cutting edges that may be inserted in most areas of the
dentition by altering and adapting the finger rest, fulcrum and hand position
of the operator, and hence called universal.
Eg - Barnhart curettes #1-2 and 5-6
Columbia curettes #13-14 and 2R-2L and 4R-4L
Basic characteristics of a curette
- spoon shaped blade and rounded tip

Gracey #7-8 and g-10 - Posterior teeth - facial and lingual
Gracey # 11-12 - Pasterior teeth -mesial surface
Gracey # 13-14 - Pasterior teeth - distal surface

TREATMENT OF PERIODONTAL ABSCESS
Periodontal abscess most often present as a painful dental emergency.
The patient should be treated immediately to relieve pain and resolve the
infection, which may spread and lead to periodontal attachment Lass.
Treatment of periodontal abscess includes 2 phases
a> resolving the acute lesion, and
b> management of resulting chronic condition
TREATMENT OPTIONS FOR PERIODONTAL ABSCESS
A> Drainage through pocket retraction
arincision
B> Scaling and root planning
c> Periodontal surgery
D> Systemic antibiotics
E> Tooth removal
A> ACUTE ABSCESS
The acute abscess is treated to alleviate symptoms, control the spreadof
infection, and establish drainage.
Before treatment, patients medical history, dental history, and systemic
conditions are reviewed to determine the need for systemic antibiotics.
DRAINAGE THROLIGH POCKET RETRACTION
The peripheral area around the abscess is anaesthetised and the pocket wall
is gently retracted with a periodontal probe and curette to initiate drainage
through the pocket entrance.
If the lesion is small and access is uncomplicated, debridement in the form
of scaling and root plaxing may be undertaken.
If the Lesion is Large and drainage cannot be established, root debridement
by scaling and root planing or surgical access should be delayed until
major clinical signs have subsided.
In these pts. use of systemic antibiotics with short-term high dose
regimens is recommended.
Antibiotic therapy alone without subsequent drainage and subgingival
scaling is contraindicated.

DRAINAGE THROUGH EXTERNAL INCISION
OE
ТИЕМТАЭЯТ
The abscess is dried and isolated with gauze sponges.
Topical anaesthetic is applied followed by LA injection peripheral to the lesion.
A vertical incision through the most fluctuant center of the abscess is made
with # 15 surgical blade.
The tissue lateral to the incision can be separated with a curette or
periosteal elevator.
Fluctuant matter is expressed, the wound edges are approximated under
light digital pressure with a moist gauze pad.
For those who do not need systemic antibiotics, past treatment instructions
include frequent rinsing with warm salt water and periodic application
of chlorhexidine gluconate either by rinsing or locally with a cotton-
tipped applicator.
Reduced exertion and increased fluid intake is recommonded for systemic
involvement.

ENAP
ЗИОПАЖИ!
The Excisional New Attachment Procedure [ENAP] is a definitive subgingival
curettage procedure performed with a knife.
It has been developed by US Naval Dental Corps.
Subgingival curettage refers to the procedure that is performed apical to the
junctional epithelium and severing the connective tissue attachment down
to the asseous crest.
PROCEDURE
1> After adequate anaesthesia, make an internal bevel incision from the
margin of the free gingiva apically to a point below the bottom of the pocket.
carry the incision interproximally on both the facial and Lingual side,
attempting to retain as much interproximal tissue as passible.
The intention is to cut the inner partion of the soft tissue wall of the pocket,
all around the tooth.
2> Remove the excised tissue with a curette, and carefully perform root
planing on all expased cementum to achieve a smooth, hard consistency.
Preserve all connective tissue fibers that remain attached to the root surface
3> Approximate the wound edges .If they do not meet passively, recontour
the bone until good adaptation of the wound edges is achieved.
Place sutures and a periodontal dressing

ANUG
Necrotising Ulcerative Gingivitis is a microbial disease of gingiva in the context
of an impaired host response. It is characterised by the necrasis and sloughing
of gingival tissues, and it presents with characteristic signs and symptoms.
It is also known as Trench mouth, vincent angina and acute ulceromembranous
gingivitis.
ETIOLOGY
caused by Borrelia vincentii and spirochetes
Predispasing factors are -
stress, immunasuppression, smoking trauma, poor oral hygiene,
inadequate sleep, down's syndrome
CLINICAL FEATURES
Males are more frequently involved
gingiva becomes red and painful
Lesions are characterised by punched-out, crater like depressions of crests
of interdental papilla
Lesions are covered by pseudomembrane
Pronounced bleeding tendency
Unpleasant fetid odar
Patient complains of constant radiating gnawing pain that is intensified
by eating spicy & hot foods.
MANAGEMENT
Ultrasonic scaling, [subgingival scaling is contraindicated]
Antibiotic therapy - -Metronidazole, Erythromycin
[ Metronidazole contraindicated in pregnancy]
Avoid alchohol & smoking
NSAIDS for pain relief - -Ibuprofen
Correction of systemic conditions
Mouth rinse with 000 0.1% CHX soln. BD.
Supportive therapy - rest, fluids intake & soft nutritious diet.
